# Transcrição: 2.8-2 Part 2 - Product Level Testing & Funnels

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Hey guys, this is Deepash. This is Facebook ads for an e-com training video. Right now, I'm going to go through the test structure for different types of e-com. So drop ship, print on demand, and also brand, and the kind of test structure that I go through with an account. Now, one of the core things I get asked a lot is the structure of an account, but then it's also the structure of the testing, and especially when it comes to products. The big part of e-com is actually finding the lead product, maybe even finding the best product. So if you've got a general store and you've got multiple products you're selling, some of them will sell by virtue of people being on the site, so you might cross sell. Others you want is your lead product. So if you imagine you've got multiple categories, jackets, dresses, shoes, whatever it is, electronics, what's your lead product and how do you go about testing that? Now, the first thing I want to just kind of reiterate and hopefully you've seen already in the training is the full funnel system. So I use the full funnel system to essentially create my account structure. When it comes to account structure, there's lots of different ways that people build their kind of work, the terminology, the campaigns, the ad sets, and the ads. And some people just throw up campaigns for every single type of targeting, and then the ad set has the same kind of details. You have one campaign, one ad set, one ad. Other people just have one campaign and like a thousand different ad sets and lots of different ads. My structure is where I try and kind of think of the customer funnel. So thinking about the prospect going from not knowing about you to knowing about you to being interested in actually buying. So the full funnel system is essentially breaking it out on a kind of thermometer gauge, going from cold, warm, hot, and then you kind of got the sales coming through, and then also existing customers. If there's one area that I see people missing out on Facebook a lot, I don't know if you can see that properly, it's existing customers. So don't think of your Facebook ads marketing just as an acquisition channel. It's also a reacquisition channel as well. So your cold audience up here, just kind of rewrite that back so it's clearer. So your cold audience is all of your prospecting. So you've got your interests, you've got your behaviors, you've got broad targeting, you've got your various demographics, you've got your lookalikes. So my cold audience pot is all about prospecting. So I want to find people in my market in my niche that are interested in my product. So I want them to come and click through. And really the goal here is to figure out how you're going to get your message across. So the full funnel system is just a structure,

## [03:01 - 06:01]

but then how you message in each part of the funnel is defined based on angles and offers. So I talk a lot about angles and offers. So recently today, we were as a team talking about a particular client, and it's in the gifting niche, and it's also in the pets niche. Pets is probably one of the most passionate niches out there. It's print on demand, and gifting is obviously huge. And right now, as I recall this, we're in Q4, we're heading into Christmas. So when you combine all those things together, it's a recipe for success. A hugely passionate niche, which is dogs, thinking about gifting, and kind of wearing the biggest gifting season of the year. So you take those things together, and then you think about angles. So the angle is obviously there's Christmas, but there's also Christmas gifts for siblings, for children, for parents, for grandparents. So you can cut those different ways. And then you think about the offer. Now, the offer doesn't have to be a discount necessarily. I talk about the offer as the thing that you want them to take advantage of. So the offer could be a particular product, and wouldn't it be amazing to have the name of your dog printed on this t-shirt, for example, but calling out the specific t-shirt. So this is another thing about targeting with your ads, especially when it comes to testing print on demand. I've seen a lot of cases where people will publish a category. So you can get your teas, and you can kind of get your print on demand teas, you can put your name on here, but sometimes it's hard for people to visualize. But if you've got a range of teas, and you're delivering each individual tea, you're actually testing to see which one is getting traction, which is getting interest, then actually leading with that tea, you want the one that's going to hook people in. So it might be a movie theme, tea, it could be some kind of word play or something like that. Then someone's like, alright, I can visualize myself with that. So with the cold audience, I'm thinking about kind of angles and offers to bring people into the journey. Now, you may have a discount, which is absolutely fine. So your offer is maybe led by 10% discount. I personally don't like to lead with an offer from my cold audience, because especially if you're trying to build something long term, you're trying to build a brand, unless that's what you're after, you're going to attract those people that are bargain hunters. So again, I see a lot of dropshippers in particular taking advice from e-com dropshippers saying, you know, just put a 10% discount by now, limited availability, all that kind of stuff. That's appealing to the functional side of the brain. The functional part of the brain actually creates barriers, so barriers to purchase. So I don't want to buy this product because it's $29.99. That's a functional reason not to buy. The emotional is you're gifting and it's Christmas and this is a great gift for Christmas, or maybe it's a bundle gift or something like that. When it's harder to actually put a tangible amount against it.

## [06:01 - 09:02]

So for example, if I'm creating a dog tea and it's related to creating memories and lasting memories or celebrating my pets or something like that, it's hard to put a tangible number behind that and therefore the brain struggles to process it. And so really when you're pushing your ads to the cold audience, you're appealing to a part of the brain that you can't consciously control as well as one that says, you know, your product is $29.99. Even if it says it's got 50% off, people are still working numbers and kind of functionally deciding whether it's right for them, but it's tangible. And that makes it easier to say yes or no. So that's just something to think about in your cold time. Now when it comes to warm, for me, I define a warm audience as one that is engaged but not clicked. So if these are my clicked audience down here, so they've visited my site, these are the people that have engaged. So it could be that they are fans. It could be that they've engaged with my Instagram page, my Facebook posts. They could have engaged with videos, for example. So when you talk about video remarketing, if you're running video ads up here, you may be going straight for sale, which is absolutely fine. But consider re-targeting those that have viewed the video but not clicked. That for me becomes a warm audience. So you're re-engaging them here. The differentiation is they know about you already. So they've already seen something about you, whether it's an ad or a post on your feed, on your page or something like that. So therefore the messaging changes here. So you're not necessarily reintroducing the same concept. For example, I'm doing print on demand teas and this is a great tea for XYZ. There are steps down from that. So they know that you're doing teas. Potentially they know that you're doing a pet's tea. For example, now what's the message here? How can you actually hook them back? So if I was going to offer a discount, then I would most like to introduce it here first, not in my cold. And then for me, the third tier actually, the hot, is people that have clicked in my site but not purchased. So generally I'd be retargeting people that have viewed a product and fired the view content event, added to cart and abandoned, for example. So here then I'm playing with dynamic product ads. I'm playing with website conversion, retargeting ads, and trying to really hook people back that have shown more of a dedicated interest. Now if I am going to actually start on a discounted journey, if that's what I want to do, I would start it down here because at this point these people are really kind of interested and they're interested for the right reason. They've gone into the product, for example, they've added to cart, but there's just something holding them back. This is where you actually start to talk about the functional benefits of your product. So again, going into the kind of teas example, if you're selling a tea shirt and you've been talking about memories and prints and name of your pet and all that kind of stuff here, all the intangible benefits, here it's about comfort and fit and things like that.

## [09:02 - 12:03]

And this is a key thing that people miss out, especially with apparel print on demand, is if you strip everything out, it's just a tea shirt. How do you sell a tea shirt online? Different people buy it for different reasons. So is it warm? Will it fit? What kind of fit is it? Is it tight? Is it loose? What kind of material is it cotton? Is it polyester? How does it wash? Can I tumble dry it? There's so many things that come out of actually buying an apparel product that people forget. There's no way to put that here because that kind of stuff is irrelevant. But then I would start to bring that here. So if you haven't purchased, what is it that's holding you back? Now you can test those things and you can maybe guess that what's holding people back. I actually prefer to run a survey. So I survey people that have viewed the product page not purchased or added to cart and not purchased and simply ask them the question, is there anything stopping you from buying today? And that gives me a ton of insight as to what's actually blocking people. Is it the price? Which means I haven't got my proposition well across. Is it availability? Maybe the size that they're after isn't available if you're talking about apparel. Or maybe the thing that you're personalizing and going into the personalization side of things. Maybe the name's too long and it just didn't appear properly. Or there could be something else about the particular product that's stopping them. And it could often be something that's not immediately obvious. So for example, on one example recently, it wasn't clear that there was free shipping. Even though there was free shipping shown in the ad and in the banner, someone had quite a few responses saying, wasn't sure about shipping costs. Now for you, you look at that and say it's in the ad, it's in the banner, it's on one of the tabs on the product page, it's on XYZ page. The user is crazy, it's right there, why they're not looking at it. Well, that's your problem because if they can't see it, that's something you've done wrong, not the user. So if the user doesn't understand your page for any reason at all, even if it's down obvious, it's in big bright red letters and you get a number of people saying that I didn't know what shipping was, you need to fix that. So that's the kind of insights I'd be using. But then I'd also use that in my retargeting. So if someone's exited the site and I know that shipping is a common question, then I'm going to create an ad here and my lead message to test that would be free shipping and then maybe bring it in some other content as well. So that's just quickly on the structure that I use for the full funnel system. But what I want to actually talk about is how I actually test my products and my categories and they're kind of set up and have in place for being able to see which of my categories and products are working. So whether you're doing drop ship, print on demand, brand or whatever kind of e-commerce you're doing, the testing and the approach is themed around graduation testing. So if you haven't been through that part of the training, I encourage you to do so. Graduation testing is all about finding winning audiences and winning creatives as fast as possible.

## [12:03 - 15:03]

But the way I structure it for products is a way that kind of makes structural sense to me. So if you think about it, the kind of campaign level, ad set level and then ad level. So that's the kind of three parts of Facebook campaign, Facebook account. What I generally do is to have my category at the campaign level, then my audience is here and then my products here. So essentially, if I take an example of, let's say, the category is shoes, for example. So let's say I've got multiple store and let's say I've got men's shoes and women's shoes. So they're very different styles, different designs, etc. And actually my campaign here, considering my kind of four funnel systems, I've got cold, warm, hot and existing customers. So my naming structure is actually based on each part of the funnel. So one, also the objective for the campaign, so website conversion in this context. And then which part of the funnel is website conversion cold. And then I might put the category in. So I'm actually going to rub this out and write it smaller so it's not going across the line that's extended that. So I've got one cold website conversion. And let's say we're doing US, for example. And then I might put men's shoes. I put that really small just to fit that in. So now I've got my category at the top level. So I know all of my testing within that is going to be men's shoes. And then I've got my audience. So everything's obviously going to be male. I might have 18 plus broad. I might have 1% purchase lookalike. I might have interest based on whatever it is. So I've got all my audience listed out there. And then what I generally do at this level is when I'm creating my ads, I create five or 10 different ads of a group of products. And I actually test the same ads and the same products in all of these ad sets. So if I've got these ads, unless there's a difference between, you know, I'm doing 18 to 25 and I'm doing sneakers. And I'm doing 55 plus and I'm doing kind of traditional shoes. If I'm doing kind of broad testing and I want to see what my lead products are, which products are working for different audiences, then at the ad level, I've got my products. So it might be, let's say, shoe one, shoe two, shoe three. And within each of those products, you've then got creative variants as well.

## [15:03 - 18:08]

So you might have one video, one static, you might test carousel, you might test collection ads, et cetera. So at this level, if you really want to extensively go through every format, every creative variant, and that includes copy as well, you're going to have a ton of things to test. I try and choose one format first and see which of those products really stand out. So the easiest thing, and it really depends on what kind of assets you've got available, is to get a static image. Start with a static image, the best static image you can create two, maybe three different copy variants so that you can kind of isolate whether it's the copy that's making the difference or not. So for example, if I've got 10 products here, and I'll just put shoe 10. So if I have 10 products here, and I want to test these 10 products out, if I've got one image for each product, so that's 10 images, but I've got two copy variants, and those copy variants come back to the angles and the offers that I'm playing on, then I'm going to test those out with every single shoe there. So now I have 20 ads that I've got. So one ad for each of the shoes, one for each image, and then two versions of the copies, that's 20 ads. Then if you want to then test other variants like video, carousel, etc., you're going to have a lot more ads. But especially when you're starting out, just keep it manageable. Don't think about your minimal viable launch, whether that's the kind of minimal viable funnel. Another thing about this kind of funnel system is I don't set everything up when I start with a brand new store. I have my cold and my retargeting, because that's all you need. You need to be doing prospecting and then retargeting. And the same thing here as well. I wouldn't launch a ton of new categories here. I would start with one or two. I would have done my audience research and have my audiences all here, and I would replicate those across different categories. I would be spending low using the graduation testing to do kind of $10, $20 a day test to see which of those products are working. So in terms of the detail behind that, that's all in the graduation testing training. But what I want to do is test small but test broad. So there's different ways of testing. If a budget is not an issue, then you can actually start doing $50 per product, per ad, sorry. You can do even $100 and get results a lot faster. You can optimize for your purchase pixel, see if you get sales through. But a lot of people don't have the budget to actually focus on testing lots of things at high budgets. So the graduation testing kind of supports both methods. If the budget is a constraint, then you can do smaller spend assets and focus on getting enough impressions through. So I generally aim for $1,500 impressions. And then see if you're getting significant data through in terms of click-through rate on your ad and can people go in through your funnel as well. If you have larger budgets, then that's great. You can test at a higher velocity. So there's two ways of looking at that. Essentially, this is the structure that I try and aim for when I'm doing my multi-category testing.

## [18:08 - 21:13]

And this works for dropship, print and demand, or branded e-com. And by the way, when people talk about branded e-com, and it's better than dropship, etc., there's a lot of branded e-com companies out there that all they do is they have a dropship type of supplier. So they're getting their products from China, except they interface the product themselves. They store it locally, run QA control over it, and then sell the product. One of the things that actually stresses me out about dropshipping, which is why I don't really have a passion or want to really do much with dropshipping. I've tried it in the past, is that policy control aspect is making sure that your customer is going to be happy and consistently happy. Because people have different views of selling products. For me, if I sell a product, I want something I would be happy with as well. And certainly with dropshipping, it's harder, especially if you're doing it from China, to get consistent quality. If you're doing it local, sometimes you can get way better control. Print on demand has far more chance of quality, especially if it's a reputable print-on-demand supplier. But a lot of brands out there, all they're doing is interfacing the Chinese supplier with their own Q&A, with their own warehouse. They'll reject products that have processes in place. And then what gets to customers is high quality. So when a lot of people not dropshipping, I think they're not really understanding the fact that dropshipping is great for testing. It's great for growing revenues and growing a good part of revenue. If you want to create a brand from that, get the products shipped to you locally, build your own fulfillment center, and build your own quality process. And you will see that the extra money that you're actually putting in to develop a consistent experience can help you to actually create a brand. And when you have a brand, you can also charge more as well. So when I see people knocking dropshipping, I don't think that's the right way of taking it. Dropshipping is great for testing. So for example, I spoke recently about an Amazon seller who's kind of coming up with product ideas and having to put a lot of research in to make sure that the products getting shipped to the Amazon fulfillment center, 1000 items, 2000, whatever it is, are pretty much going to sell. Because if they don't sell, then you'll start with products in the warehouse and you don't know what to do with it. Dropshipping again can help you with that. So set up a quick shop if you buy a store, start testing products out at low cost and see which ones people are actually buying. Start surveying them. Why did you buy it? Where else would you look? Would you consider Amazon? Start collecting that data. And then when you're confident enough in a product, then you order a pallet in, send it to Amazon and start selling. So there's different ways of using dropshipping, different ways of using print on demand. As an example also at a mastermind I was out recently, someone had a dropshipped product and they said, look, I don't really know how to monetize my existing customers. So I talk a lot about upping your lifetime value. When you come to Ecom, yes, you know, CPMs are getting more expensive. It's harder and harder, certainly over the last two, three years,

## [21:13 - 24:17]

to get really low CPAs consistently. So if you're in a market where you can't get your AOV up, and I would consider a difficult AOV, anything up to $35, $40 would be quite difficult to get more than a two return on Aspendom. And if you're struggling to get your AOV up, the other option is to get your lifetime value up. And so this is where marketing to existing customers comes in. There's different ways of doing that. There's email, there's messenger, there's Facebook ads, print, you know, putting some leaflets into delivery, for example. But that's not the problem. The problem is like what do you sell them? So some products you sell once and someone's not really going to buy from you again. Some products they will buy repeatedly. But this particular guy was saying, I've got a dropship store and there's no natural product that I can up or sell, up or cross sell them into. So my suggestion was actually bolt on print on demand. So actually you've sold the product and, you know, there's some good ideas for his niche to bolt on products that were related to what he was selling. And actually, if you get a good print on demand supply, you get a good post purchase experience set up, you get some really good complimentary products in place. And actually you get a good amount of repurchases through those print on demand. So again, you can also merge dropship with print on demand that way as well. But you know, what I would also say is if you are looking to build a brand out, brand comes through consistency and quality. And also if you're looking to kind of build on top of dropship, I've talked about quality control. If you're looking to build a brand on print and demand, you probably want to own more of the stack. So the print on demand supplier is going to take a good chunk for the product and the film and other parts of it. If you're looking to stack up a brand on top of it, and I'm talking going from six to seven figures and seven to eight figures, then you're going to be thinking about stacking up more value internally within your business and owning more of the stack as well. So that's my kind of approach to income product category and product and kind of right down to the lowest level of testing. And hopefully that kind of gives you an idea of how to structure your test when you're actually getting started with different products, different opportunities, even if it's an existing store and you want to bolt on dropship to print on demand or vice versa, or if you've got a brand, you've got new lines coming in. This is just a good way to build up that testing. The other thing I would point out as well is there's a lot of talk on having an account per category and diluting the pixel data and confusing the pixel and stuff like that. What I will say is that the Facebook pixel is extremely, extremely clever, or at least Facebook are in the way they use that pixel data. So the first thing about that is if you have good schema and kind of metadata set up on your website, and that's something which is also covered off in the training, that tells Facebook the context of the product you're selling.

## [24:17 - 26:45]

So if you're, for example, on a default install of Shopify, then Shopify put that in themselves. And the way to check that is in the pixel helper, if you expand out and below your events, there should be a link for schema.org, I think, expand that out and there should be a lot of information. You can have a look at what is being sent out to get an idea of what kind of context is sent. Because when that context is sent to Facebook, Facebook are learning what the product is about and matching users. Now, one of the other advantages of having a category per campaign is also the campaign level holds history and data on the users, the performers, etc. And so by bundling your categories at this level and kind of creating relevance specific to that, your assets are picking up relevant data. So in this particular instance, it's all about mail. So Facebook will now know that this is all about mail targeting and specifically about this category, because it's all about shoes, all the products are shoes, all the data being sent back from your website via the pixel is all shoe-related. And this is why often broad targeting works so well. This is something that Facebook are really trying to push people towards. Something to test, so broad targeting versus defined targeting, because if Facebook know the context of your site is shoes, the objective of your website conversion campaign is to get purchases and your targeting men, Facebook kind of know who's in the market. So if your browsing website, you're looking for shoes and the Facebook pixel data is installed, Facebook's collecting that data at the user level. So it knows that you are now in the market for shoes. And therefore, if you're looking for people that are interested in purchasing, then it will kind of try to serve you. Adds up, if you're not in the market for shoes, but you're fitting in that profile. And what Facebook also do is people that have recently bought shoes, they have their own data in their background. So they know that these are the profiles of men 18 plus they're in the market for shoes. It kind of almost creates a hidden look alike that you can't see because it finds more people likely to buy your products as well. So they're just different ways to make your product work. Facebook has far more data than I think people realize. But the big part of supporting that is working on the right structure for your account, but then also at the campaign level to feed that data.
